name: zustand
summary: 🐻 Bear necessities for state management in React

story.problem: Redux solves state management but brings enormous complexity: actions, reducers, selectors, middleware, and a steep learning curve for what is often a shared variable. Context API re-renders the entire subtree on every update. Zustand was built to provide global state with the simplicity of useState and the power of Redux.
story.mental_model: A Zustand store is a custom hook. You define state and actions together in one object, and any component calls useStore(state => state.count) to subscribe to exactly the slice it needs. Only components that subscribed to changed state re-render. No providers, no reducers, no boilerplate.
story.when_to_use: Use Zustand for global UI state that multiple components need to read and write: auth status, theme preferences, shopping cart, modal state, or any data too complex for prop drilling but not complex enough to justify Redux.
story.when_not_to_use: Zustand is not designed for server state — use React Query for that. For very large applications with complex state machines or time-travel debugging requirements, Redux Toolkit's tooling is more mature.

alternatives.name: jotai
alternatives.reason: Atomic state model — each atom is independent, avoiding the single-store pattern; better for derived state

alternatives.name: valtio
alternatives.reason: Proxy-based state that feels like mutating plain objects — very natural if you dislike the selector pattern

alternatives.name: redux-toolkit
alternatives.reason: More opinionated but better DevTools, time-travel debugging, and team conventions for large apps

nodes.label: zustand
nodes.summary: Import as `import { create } from 'zustand'`. Minimal, unopinionated state management.

nodes.label: create(initializer)
nodes.summary: Factory that returns a React hook: `const useStore = create((set, get) => ({ count: 0, inc: () => set(s => ({count: s.count+1})) }))`.

nodes.label: useStore(selector?)
nodes.summary: React hook to access store state. `const count = useStore(s => s.count)`. Re-renders only when selection changes.

nodes.label: set(partial | updater)
nodes.summary: Update state. Partial: `set({ x: 1 })`. Updater: `set(state => ({ x: state.x + 1 }))`. Does shallow merge.

nodes.label: get()
nodes.summary: Read current state inside actions. Useful when you need current state inside an action closure.

nodes.label: store.subscribe(listener)
nodes.summary: Subscribe to state changes outside React. Returns unsubscribe fn. Used in non-component code.

nodes.label: immer middleware
nodes.summary: `create(immer(initializer))` — write mutable-style updates inside set. Uses Immer under the hood.

nodes.label: devtools middleware
nodes.summary: `create(devtools(initializer))` — connect to Redux DevTools for time-travel debugging.

nodes.label: shallow (equality)
nodes.summary: import shallow from 'zustand/shallow'. Use as second arg to useStore to prevent unnecessary re-renders when selecting multiple values.

steps.label: Import
steps.code: If you want to construct a single object with multiple state-picks inside, similar to redux's mapStateToProps, you can use [useShallow](./docs/guides/prevent-rerenders-with-use-shallow.md) to prevent unnecessary rerenders when the selector output does not change according to shallow equal.

steps.label: Step 2
steps.code: For more control over re-rendering, you may provide any custom equality function (this example requires the use of [`createWithEqualityFn`](./docs/migrations/migrating-to-v5.md#using-custom-equality-functions-such-as-shallow)).

steps.label: Import
steps.code: You can use a vanilla store with `useStore` hook available since v4.

tasks.title: Can't live without redux-like reducers and action types?

steps.label: Import
steps.code: Or, just use our redux-middleware. It wires up your main-reducer, sets the initial state, and adds a dispatch function to the state itself and the vanilla API.
